page.content: The analysis of the impact of sex and age on health outcomes is critical for understanding population dynamics and tailoring health interventions. The data presented in the accompanying table provides valuable insights into the performance metrics associated with these demographic factors. Specifically, the table highlights the Area Under the Curve (AUC) value of 0.637, which indicates a moderate level of discrimination in predicting health outcomes based on sex and age.

lines.text: The analysis of the impact of sex and age on health outcomes is critical for understanding population dynamics and tailoring
health interventions. The data presented in the accompanying table provides valuable insights into the performance metrics
associated with these demographic factors. Specifically, the table highlights the Area Under the Curve (AUC) value of 0.637,
which indicates a moderate level of discrimination in predicting health outcomes based on sex and age.

Adjusted for sex, age and multimorbidity

page.content: The 95% Confidence Interval (95% CI) for this AUC, ranging from 0.632 to 0.642, further reinforces the reliability of this estimate, suggesting that the model is statistically robust and provides a stable assessment of the predictive capability. Additionally, the dataset encompasses a substantial sample size of 74,959 individuals, enhancing the validity and generalizability of the findings. The percentage of variance explained by these demographic factors, represented as 4.9%, underscores the importance of considering sex and age in health-related research and policy-making. These metrics collectively indicate that while sex and age are significant contributors to health outcomes, there remains an opportunity to explore additional variables that may further refine predictive models and improve health interventions across diverse populations.
